fields.name: 17. Adjudication in Debate
fields.content-01:
1. Introduction (5 minutes)

‍

  • Welcome participants and introduce the topic of adjudication.
  • Explain the importance of objective decision-making and constructive feedback in debates.
  • Share the session objectives and what participants can expect to learn.

‍

2. Discussion: Key Judging Criteria (15 minutes)

‍

  • Facilitate a group discussion on the key criteria for judging debates:
    • Argumentation: Quality, clarity, and relevance of arguments.
    • Rebuttal: How well debaters address opposing arguments.
    • Delivery: Clarity, engagement, and persuasive delivery.
    • Strategy: Use of time, overall structure, and decision-making strategy.
  • Write key points on a whiteboard or flip chart for reference.
  • Encourage questions and share examples of each criterion in action.

‍

3. Activity 1: Judging Criteria Breakdown (20 minutes)

‍

Step 1: Provide a Debate Video (3 minutes)

  • Show a 3-4 minute video clip of a debate (can be pre-recorded or live).

Step 2: Judging Exercise (12 minutes)

  • Instruct participants to evaluate the debate using the following categories:
    • Argumentation
    • Style 
    • Strategy
  • Participants should rate each category from 1 to 5 (1 being poor, 5 being excellent).
  • Provide them with a Judging Criteria Worksheet to complete as they watch the video.

Step 3: Group Reflection (5 minutes)

  • After watching the video, discuss with the group:
    • What criteria were most challenging to assess?
    • Did everyone rate the debate the same way? Why or why not?

‍

4. Activity 2: Providing Constructive Feedback (25 minutes)

‍

Step 1: Review Feedback Templates (5 minutes)

  • Introduce the Feedback Template that participants will use to give constructive feedback.
  • Discuss how to provide balanced feedback (positive, improvement areas, suggestions).

Step 2: Feedback Exercise (15 minutes)

  • Have each participant write feedback for a debater from the video using the Feedback Template.
  • Ensure that feedback addresses each of the following:
    • What was done well.
    • Areas for improvement.
    • Suggestions for improvement.

Step 3: Share Feedback (5 minutes)

  • Participants share one point from their feedback with the group.
  • Discuss the importance of positive reinforcement and actionable suggestions in feedback.

‍

5. Practice Judging in Pairs or Small Groups (15 minutes)

‍

Step 1: Pair or Group Participants (2 minutes)

  • Split participants into pairs or small groups of 3.

Step 2: Mini Debate (8 minutes)

  • Have each pair/group conduct a mini-debate on a provided motion (e.g., “This House believes that schools should eliminate exams.”).
  • Each participant should take turns acting as the debater and the judge.
  • Judges will use the Judging Criteria Worksheet to evaluate the debate.

Step 3: Debrief (5 minutes)

  • After each mini-debate, the judges provide feedback to the debaters.
  • Discuss the feedback process, focusing on how to deliver constructive and fair assessments.

‍

6. Wrap-Up and Q&A (10 minutes)

‍

  • Summarize the key points of the session:
    • The importance of clear, objective judging.
    • How to structure constructive feedback.
    • The impact of feedback on debater improvement.
  • Open the floor for questions.
  • Encourage participants to practice judging and providing feedback in future debates.
fields.content-02:
Objectives

‍

  • Understand the key criteria used to adjudicate debates, including argumentation, delivery, and strategy.
  • Learn how to make fair and objective decisions during judging.
  • Develop skills for providing constructive, actionable feedback to debate
fields.content-03:
Learning outcomes

‍

By the end of the session, participants will be able to:

  1. Apply judging criteria to evaluate debates fairly and objectively.
  2. Make balanced decisions when judging a debate, considering all criteria.
  3. Provide constructive feedback that helps debaters improve.
fields.content-04:
Materials and tools

‍

  • Judging criteria handout (includes categories such as argumentation, delivery, and rebuttals).
  • Example debate videos for analysis.
  • Feedback templates for providing constructive comments.
  • Timer or stopwatch for timing debates and speeches.
fields.content-05:
Assessment

‍

  • Formative Assessment: Continuous observation of participants' ability to apply the judging criteria and provide constructive feedback during exercises.
  • Peer Review: After the mini-debate, peers will evaluate each other’s feedback and judging, helping to reinforce learning and highlight areas for improvement.
  • Self-Reflection: At the end of the session, participants will fill out a self-assessment to reflect on their understanding of the judging process and feedback techniques.

‍

Tips and tricks

‍

  • Be objective: Always separate personal opinions from your judgment. Focus strictly on the content, structure, and delivery of arguments.
  • Stay calm under pressure: As a judge, you may face debates where emotions run high or arguments seem intense. Always stick to the criteria and avoid getting caught up in the drama of the debate.
  • Use specific examples: When providing feedback, give clear, specific examples to illustrate your points. This helps debaters understand exactly what to work on.
  • Balance positive and constructive feedback: Ensure that feedback is motivating and fair. Acknowledge what went well while also suggesting concrete ways to improve.
  • Practice makes perfect: The more you practice judging debates, the better you'll become at evaluating arguments and delivering feedback quickly and effectively. Consider watching debates outside of workshops to sharpen your skills.
fields.extra-text:
Appendix 1

‍

Judging Criteria Worksheet

Criteria Rating (1–5) Notes / Justification
Argumentation & Rebuttal
Quality of arguments Did the speaker provide clear, relevant, and logical arguments? How strong was the reasoning?
Rebuttal effectiveness How effectively did the speaker respond to opposing arguments? Were the rebuttals well-supported?
Style
Delivery (clarity, engagement, persuasiveness) Was the speech clear, engaging, and persuasive? How well did the speaker connect with the audience?
Language and tone Was the language appropriate and persuasive? Did the tone support the argument effectively?
Strategy
Use of time Did the speaker manage their speaking time effectively? Did they prioritize key points?
Structure and organisation Was the speech well-structured with a clear introduction, body, and conclusion? Did they use transitions effectively?
Overall strategy Did the speaker effectively structure their argumentation to create a strong overall case?

Rating scale

  • 1 — Poor: Major issues with argumentation, delivery, or strategy.
  • 2 — Fair: Some strong points but overall lacks coherence or clarity.
  • 3 — Good: Solid performance with clear arguments and effective delivery.
  • 4 — Very good: Well-structured, persuasive, and strategically sound.
  • 5 — Excellent: Outstanding argumentation, delivery, and strategic use of time.

‍

Appendix 2

‍

Feedback Template

‍

What Was Done Well:

[Provide specific examples of strengths in argumentation, delivery, or strategy.]

‍

Areas for Improvement:

[Provide constructive criticism about specific aspects, such as rebuttal quality, clarity of delivery, or argument development.]

‍

Suggestions for Improvement:

[Offer actionable steps for improvement, such as practicing rebuttals, working on body language, or developing stronger evidence.]
